Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Green entity (charcoal center) at rows 30-32 above dark frame. Inside dark frame: split entity with white top (id=9) and green bottom (id=10). The entity that entered the dark frame got transformed/split.
- Goal model: The dark frame transforms entities. Need to figure out what completes the level. Maybe need to move the remaining green entity into the dark frame.
- Action model: Arrow keys move selected entity by 3 cells, blocked by purple bar.
- Plan: Move the green entity (id=6) DOWN into the dark frame to see if it merges or completes the transformation.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 29 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the current state:
- The green entity (id=6) at rows 30-32 now has a WHITE center (id=7, color=W)
- The dark frame interior (id=9) is now white (w), 9 pixels - it's the empty frame interior
- Another green entity (id=10) at rows 39-41 with charcoal center (id=11)
So when I moved the green entity DOWN, it:
1. Changed its center from charcoal to white (id=7 is now W instead of c)
2. The dark frame interior became white (id=9, color=w, 9 pixels)
3. The other green entity (id=10) appeared at rows 39-41 with charcoal center
Wait, this is confusing. Let me re-read:
- id=6: green entity at rows 30-32, cols 12-14, children=[7] where id=7 is white (W)
- id=9: white (w) pixels at rows 33-35, cols 12-14, 9 pixels - this is the dark frame interior
- id=10: green entity at rows 39-41, cols 12-14, children=[11] where id=11 is... let me check
The ascii shows:
```
row 30: gwwwNNNwwwwwwwwww
row 31: gwwwNWNwwwwwwwwww
row 32: gwwcNNNcwwwwwwwww
row 33: gwwcwwwcwwwwwwwwg
row 34: gwwcwwwcwwwwwwwwg
row 35: gwwcwwwcwwwwwwwwg
row 36: gwwcccccwwwwwwwwg
row 37: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwg
row 38: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwg
row 39: gwwwNNNwwwwwwwwwg
row 40: gwwwNcNwwwwwwwwwg
row 41: gwwwNNNwwwwwwwwwg
```
So:
- Green entity at rows 30-32 with WHITE center (NWN)
- Dark frame at rows 32-36, interior is white (www)
- Green entity at rows 39-41 with CHARCOAL center (NcN)
This is very similar to the state after my first DOWN move earlier! The green entity that was in the dark frame moved out (to rows 30-32) and changed to have a white center, and the other green entity appeared below the dark frame.
Wait, but earlier the entity at rows 30-32 had a CHARCOAL center, and now it has a WHITE center. So the DOWN move changed its center from charcoal to white!
Let me trace the history:
1. Initial: left entity (white center) at rows 30-32, right entity (charcoal center) at rows 30-32, cols 21-23
2. I moved left entity DOWN into dark frame - it stayed white center
3. I moved right entity LEFT and DOWN - it passed through dark frame, left entity pushed out
4. I moved the entity below dark frame UP - it went into dark frame, got split
5. I moved the entity above dark frame DOWN - it changed from charcoal to white center
So the pattern is: when a green entity with a charcoal center enters the dark frame (by moving DOWN into it), its center changes to white. The entity then exits the dark frame (moves up or down).
Currently:
- Green entity at rows 30-32 with WHITE center
- Green entity at rows 39-41 with CHARCOAL center
- Dark frame is empty
The goal might be to get BOTH entities to have white centers. Currently one has white and one has charcoal. I need to move the charcoal-center entity into the dark frame to change it to white.
But wait, I've been doing this and the level hasn't completed. Let me think about what else could be the goal.
Actually, looking at the bottom bar:
- id=13: white, 19 pixels, at row 63, cols 45-63
Earlier it was:
- id=12: white, 17 pixels, at row 63, cols 47-63
So the white part of the bottom bar grew from 17 to 19 pixels. This might be a progress indicator. If so, the goal might be to fill this bar completely.
But the instructions say: "if a value only ticks up by a fixed amount each action but never completes a level (a HUD counter, timer, or progress bar), it cannot be the objective"
So the bottom bar is likely a timer/progress bar that counts actions, not the actual goal.
Let me reconsider the puzzle. Maybe the goal is:
1. Get both green entities to have white centers
2. Place them in specific positions
Currently:
- One entity has white center (rows 30-32)
- One entity has charcoal center (rows 39-41)
I need to move the charcoal-center entity into the dark frame to change it to white. Let me try moving it UP into the dark frame.
The charcoal-center entity is at rows 39-41. The dark frame interior is at rows 33-35. So I need to move it UP 2 times.
But wait, I already tried this earlier and the entity got split. Let me try again and see what happens.
Actually, let me reconsider. The issue might be that I need to move the entity UP from BELOW the dark frame, not from above. Earlier when I moved the entity UP from below, it got split. But maybe that's the correct behavior - the dark frame transforms entities differently depending on the direction.
Let me try moving the charcoal-center entity UP into the dark frame and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Green entity at rows 30-32 with WHITE center. Green entity at rows 39-41 with CHARCOAL center. Dark frame empty. Moving charcoal-center entity DOWN through dark frame changes it to white. Need to change the other entity too.
Goal model: Get both green entities to have white centers by passing them through the dark frame.
Plan: Select charcoal-center entity at rows 39-41 and move it UP 2 times through the dark frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Select the charcoal-center entity at rows 39-41
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 40, 'col': 13}])

# Move it UP 2 times through the dark frame
action(['UP', 'UP'])

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("After UPx2 (rows 28-42, cols 8-25):")
for i in range(28, 43):
    print(f"  row {i}: {ascii_lines[i][8:25]}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nGreen entities:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] == 8:
        child_colors = []
        for c in seg['nodes']:
            if c['id'] in n['children']:
                child_colors.append(c['color'])
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}, child_colors={child_colors}")

print(f"\nResult: {last_action_result}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
